summary: A 62-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes (15 years), hypertension, and a recent eGFR of 38 mL/min/1.73m^2 with UACR 350 mg/g asks her primary care nurse whether she really needs a nephrology referral or whether her primary clinician can manage this. The held-out answer must reflect KDIGO 2024 CKD guideline staging (G3b A3 in this case), the referral thresholds (eGFR <30 / G4 generally referred; earlier referral if rapid decline, severe albuminuria like A3, refractory hypertension, suspected non-DM cause, or anemia/mineral-bone-disease), the role of ACEi/ARB titration for albuminuria, the role of SGLT2 inhibitors and finerenone in slowing CKD progression in DM (CREDENCE / DAPA-CKD / EMPA-KIDNEY / FIDELIO-DKD), and the importance of avoiding nephrotoxins.

messages.content: I'm 62, diabetic for 15 years, blood pressure runs high. My latest labs showed an eGFR of 38 and protein in the urine — they said the ratio was 350. My primary care said maybe a kidney doctor referral. Is that necessary, or can my primary doctor handle this?

do_NOT: describe eGFR 38 with UACR 350 as not warranting nephrology involvement
describe ACEi/ARB as optional in this profile
ignore the SGLT2 inhibitor / finerenone evidence for slowing CKD progression in DM
describe nephrology referral as a sign of failure
skip the avoid-nephrotoxin / vaccine / lifestyle recommendations

expected_sources_referenced: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD — Kidney Int 2024
